pattern: Since the 'up' move's jump strictly increases and its size doubles each use, only a handful of ups (roughly log2 k of them) can ever matter -- once you fix how many ups a path uses, where its 'down' moves sit becomes a pure counting question.
hints: Every 'up' uses jump values 0, 1, 2, ... in that fixed order, so if a path uses exactly t up-moves in total, they contribute a fixed displacement of 2^t - 1 regardless of where the downs are interspersed.

The t up-moves split the operation sequence into t+1 slots (before the first up, between each consecutive pair, and after the last); at most one down can sit in any single slot, since two downs with nothing between them would be consecutive.

For a fixed number of ups t, the final position is 2^t - d where d is how many of those t+1 slots hold a down; solve d = 2^t - k for each t, and whenever 0 <= d <= t+1 add C(t+1, d) to the answer -- stop increasing t once 2^t - k exceeds t+1, since the gap only widens from there.

edge_inputs.description: smallest stair beyond the two worked examples
a stair reachable by exactly one very direct path
a stair unreachable by any combination of ups and downs
a mid-size stair with several qualifying up-counts
k exactly one below a power of two
k exactly a power of two

stress.description: random k across the full range, checked against a bounded operation-sequence search for small k
stress.note: kept small so the bounded DFS oracle stays fast

stress.description: random large k up to the real constraint
stress.note: exercises the closed-form counting at realistic scale
